coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos in peach based on cobalt-graphene nanohybrid with excellent oxidase-like activity and reusability
coredata.dc:creator: Nana, Li
Ruiyi, Li
Qinsheng, Wang
Yongqiang, Yang
Xiulan, Sun
Guangli, Wang
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Cobalt nanocrystal has been widely used as nano-enzyme for sensing and catalysis due to its high stability and low cost, but poor catalytic activity limits its applications in bioanalysis. The study reports one strategy for synthesis of cobalt-graphene nanohybrid. Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His-GQD) was bound to graphene sheet via π-π stacking and then combined with cobalt ions in the presence of cetyltrimethylammonium chloride to form stable complex and finally reduced under nitrogen to obtain Co-His-GQD-G. The as-synthesized nanohybrid offers well-defined three-dimensional structure and quasi-superparamagnetism. The cobalt nanoparticles were well dispersed on graphene sheets. The unique structure improves oxidase-like activity of Co-His-GQD-G. Further, Co-His-GQD-G was used as the nanozyme for colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos. Co-His-GQD-G catalyzes oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine into blue product. Thiocholine produced by hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine under catalysis of acetylcholinesterase inhibits catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G and leads to a reduced oxidization rate. Chlorpyrifos inhibits activity of acetylcholinesterase and brings an enhanced absorbance of blue product. The absorbance at 652 nm linearly increases with increasing chlorpyrifos concentration in the range of 2–20 ng mL−1 with detection limit of 0.57 ng mL−1 (S/N = 3). The method was successfully applied in determination of chlorpyrifos in peach by preparing Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos in peach based on cobalt-graphene nanohybrid with excellent oxidase-like activity and reusability

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cobalt nanocrystal has been widely used as nano-enzyme for sensing and catalysis due to its high stability and low cost, but poor catalytic activity limits its applications in bioanalysis. The study reports one strategy for synthesis of cobalt-graphene nanohybrid. Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (His-GQD) was bound to graphene sheet via π-π stacking and then combined with cobalt ions in the presence of cetyltrimethylammonium chloride to form stable complex and finally reduced under nitrogen to obtain Co-His-GQD-G. The as-synthesized nanohybrid offers well-defined three-dimensional structure and quasi-superparamagnetism. The cobalt nanoparticles were well dispersed on graphene sheets. The unique structure improves oxidase-like activity of Co-His-GQD-G. Further, Co-His-GQD-G was used as the nanozyme for colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos. Co-His-GQD-G catalyzes oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine into blue product. Thiocholine produced by hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine under catalysis of acetylcholinesterase inhibits catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G and leads to a reduced oxidization rate. Chlorpyrifos inhibits activity of acetylcholinesterase and brings an enhanced absorbance of blue product. The absorbance at 652 nm linearly increases with increasing chlorpyrifos concentration in the range of 2–20 ng mL−1 with detection limit of 0.57 ng mL−1 (S/N = 3). The method was successfully applied in determination of chlorpyrifos in peach by preparing Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides are widely used in pest control and crop management in agricultural production (Huang et al., 2019). Overuse of organophosphorus pesticides will cause water, soil and air pollution, and have adverse effects on aquatic organisms, birds and ultimately human being (Kovida et al., 2020). The investigations demonstrated that organophosphorus pesticides inhibited the catalytic activity of acetylcholinesterase by irreversibly binding to active sites of acetylcholinesterase. Accumulation of neurotransmitter acetylcholine and overstimulation of synaptic transmission may lead to headache, vomiting, muscle paralysis and even death (Liu and Wei, 2014). It is of great significance to develop a highly reliable method for the determination of organophosphorus pesticides in fresh food.

The analytical techniques of organophosphorus pesticides mainly include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Wang et al., 2020a), gas chromatography (GC) (Balsini and Parastar, 2019), gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Kang et al., 2020), surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) (Sukmanee et al., 2017), fluorescence (Liang and Han, 2020), electrochemical (Liu et al., 2020) and colorimetric method (Yan et al., 2017). HPLC and GC/MS are commonly used methods for detection of organophosphorus pesticides due to their high sensitivity and reliability. However, its application in rapid on-site detection and high-throughput screening is limited by cumbersome sample pretreatment, expensive instrument and skilled operator (Yang et al., 2020). SERS has high sensitivity and selectivity, but the instability of detection signal limits its practical application (Chu et al., 2019). Fluorescent method offers rapid response, high sensitivity and favorable selectivity. However, some biological macromolecules in food can also emit the fluorescence under ultraviolet irradiation. This may cause serious background fluorescence interference when it is used in the detection of organophosphorus pesticides at a very low level (Chu et al., 2019). Electrochemical sensor gives good sensitivity and rapidity, but instability is its inherent weakness (Jie et al., 2017). Colorimetric method receives a more attention due to its low cost, simple operation and visual readout (Huang et al., 2019). Several nanomaterials, such as gold nanocrystals, have been applied as the metal nanozyme for colorimetric detection of organophosphorus pesticides. However, poor catalytic activity limits its wide applications in fresh foods (Hu et al., 2019).

The hybridization of different materials is an effective way to improve the catalytic activity of metal nanocrystals (Ko et al., 2019). Wang and co-workers reported an Au-Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles. The Au-Fe3O4 offers good peroxidase-like activity, which is 1.3-fold than that of single Au nanocrystals and 5.2-fold than that of single Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Wang et al., 2016). Futhermore, the magnetic properties of Au-Fe3O4 nanoparticles enable them to be separated from the sample solution by magnetic separation, thus reducing the spectral interference caused by sample color and turbidity of the sample in colorimetric determination (Zhao et al., 2003). Meanwhile, it also realizes the recovery and recycling of nanomaterials. Recently, hybrid of metal nanomaterial with graphene has attracted a more attention in material science (Zhang et al., 2014). Compared with metal oxide, graphene has a much better electron conductivity and specific surface area. These characteristics help to improve the catalytic activity of metal nanomaterials (Song et al., 2010). The studies show that metal-graphene nanohybrid can offer high catalytic activity and structural stability. Owing to low cost, strong magnetism, high catalase-like and peroxidase-like activity, cobalt-graphene nanohybrid has been investigated as an artificial enzyme (Mu et al., 2012). Zhang and co-workers reported synthesis of porous Co3O4 nanorod-graphene composite by hydrothermal method. The nanohybrid displays high peroxidase-like activity and thermal stability (Zhang et al., 2013). Although great progress has been made, the synthesis of ideal cobalt-graphene magnetic nanohybrid with good dispersion and catalytic activity still faces great challenges up to now.

In the study, we report synthesis of cobalt-histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-graphene nanohybrid (Co-His-GQD-G). His-GQD and cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC) were used to improve the dispersion of cobalt nanocrystals on the graphene sheets and the exposure of high-index facets. The as-synthesized Co-His-GQD-G offers excellent oxidase-like activity and quasi-superparamagnetic behavior, and has been successfully applied to the colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos. The analytical method provides the advantages of sensitivity, rapidity and stability compared with the reported colorimetric method for the determination of organophosphorus pesticides.

Graphite oxide (GO) (0.24 g) was dispersed in ultrapure water (50 mL) under ultrasonic treatment. His-GQD aqueous solution (6 mg mL−1, 50 mL) and CTAC (0.3 g) were added with stirring to form His-GQD-GO composite. After adjusting the pH of the above mixed solution to 7.0 by adding 1 M NaOH aqueous solution, CoCl2 solution (0.1 M, 12 mL) was added into the above solution to produce stable Co-His-GQD-GO complex. Followed by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min, washed three times with ultrapure water and dried at 80 °C for 4 h. The collected precipitate was reduced in N2 at 600 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 1 °C min−1 to obtain Co-His-GQD-G. The procedure was also used for the synthesis of Co-His-GQD except without GO.

The 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) aqueous solution (100 μL, 1 mM) was mixed with acetate buffer (700 μL, pH 4.0). Co-His-GQD-G or other catalyst solution (200 μL, 1 mg mL−1) was added and stirred for 1 min under vortex oscillation. To measure the oxidase-like activity, the absorbance at 652 nm of the solution was measured on the spectrophotometer after the Co-His-GQD-G in the system was rapidly removed by magnet.

The steady-state kinetic behavior of TMB oxidation catalyzed by Co-His-GQD-G or other catalyst was studied by changing the concentration of TMB. The kinetic parameters were determined by the double reciprocal form of Michaelis equation: Lineweaver-Burk model (1) (Dong et al., 2012), where V is the initial reaction rate, Vmax is maximum reaction rate, C is substrate concentration,and KM is the Michaelis constant. First, the absorbance values at 652 nm at different reaction times were measured. Then, the absorbance value was substituted into the Beer-Lambert formula to calculate the concentration of the blue oxidation product of TMB (ox-TMB). The initial reaction rate V of different reaction system is equal to the ratio of the corresponding ox-TMB concentration to the reaction time. Based on the V value of different reaction system, the Vmax and KM can be calculated from the Lineweaver-Burk equation.

The chlorpyrifos standard solution (100 μL) with different concentration or sample solution was mixed with acetylcholinesterase solution (100 μL, 20 mU mL−1) and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Acetylthiocholine chloride (100 μL, 1 mM) was added with shaking and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. After that, a mixed solution composing of TMB (100 μL, 1 mM), acetate buffer of pH 4.0 (400 μL) and Co-His-GQD-G (200 μL, 1 mg mL -1) was added to the above solution. The obtained solution was placed at room temperature for 1 min, rapidly remove the Co-His-GQD-G from the system by magnet, and measured the absorption spectrum or absorbance at 652 nm on the spectrophotometer.

Agarose (20 mg) was dissolved in 2 mL boiling water. The Co-His-GQD-G dispersion (2 mL, 10 mg mL−1) was quickly added into the solution when the agarose solution was cooled to about 50 °C. Transfer 50 μL of the mixed solution and drop it onto the plastic plate. The Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet was formed when cooled to room temperature.

All peach samples were purchased from local supermarkets in Wuxi. The peach after enucleation was cleaned with ultrapure water, dried at room temperature and then ground into juice using a household homogenizer. The juice (10 g) was mixed with the composing of the PBS of pH 7.4 (9 mL, 0.1 M) and acetone (1 mL). Followed by ultrasound for 10 min and centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The collected supernatant was used for detection of chlorpyrifos.

Different concentrations of chlorpyrifos standard solution were added to the freshly prepared sample solution. Acetylcholinesterase (50 μL, 20 mU mL−1) was added to sample solution (50 μL) containing different concentrations of chlorpyrifos and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Acetylthiocholine chloride (50 μL, 1 mM) was added and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. Subsequently, TMB (50 μL, 1 mM) and acetate buffer (300 μL, pH 4.0) were added. The above mixed solution was used as the reaction solution and dripped into the microwell plate. The Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet adsorbed on the surface of the electromagnet was placed in the reaction solution on the microwell plate. Adjust the applied current on the electromagnet to 0, the gel sheet was immersed in the reaction solution. After incubation at room temperature, the gel sheet in the reaction solution was removed by applying a current on the electromagnet. The concentration of chlorpyrifos in the sample solution was determined by measuring the absorbance of reaction solution.

Co-His-GQD-G was designed and synthesized by using a procedure described in Fig. 1. In the design, CTAC was used as the surfactant to form micelles. These micelles act as a soft template for formation of Co-His-GQD-GO complex. The template facilitates the interlacing of multiple graphene sheets to form a network structure due to the short distance between different graphene sheets. His-GQD plays a key role in the immobilization of Co2+ on the surface of graphene sheets. This helps to reduce the particle size of Co nanoparticles and improve the dispersibility of Co nanoparticles on graphene sheets. Firstly, His-GQD was firmly fixed on the graphene sheet via strong π-π stacking to form His-GQD-GO composite. Then, His-GQD in the composite was coordinated with Co2+ to form stable Co-His-GQD-GO complex. The coordination not only realizes the immobilization of Co2+ on the graphene sheet, but also leads to the rapid polymerization of graphene sheets in the CTAC micelles. Next, the Co-His-GQD-GO was thermally reduced in N2 at 600 °C for 2 h to obtain Co-His-GQD-G. In the process, most of His-GQD was decomposed and produce a large amount of reducing gases. These gases reduce Co2+ ion in the complex to Co atom, finally resulting in formation of Co nanocrystals. Because of the strong coordination bond between Co2+ and imidazole group, Co2+ was slowly released from the complex. The slow release of Co2+ is conducive to formation of the Co nanoparticles with small size and high crystallinity. The part of His-GQD were adsorbed on the surface of Co nanocrystals. The controlled growth of Co nanocrystals can be realized by using different adsorption capacities of His-GQD on different crystal faces. The resulted Co nanocrystals may exhibit enhanced catalytic activity due to their more exposed high-index facets. The most functional groups on the surface of GO and His-GQD will be stripped. With the reduction of graphene, graphene skeleton with high conductivity was formed. The resulting three-dimensional structure improves the electron/ion conductivity.

The as-synthesized Co-His-GQD-G was characterized by SEM, TEM, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), two-dimensional elemental mapping, XRD, Raman spectrum and XPS techniques. Co-His-GQD-G offers a well-defined three-dimensional structure, in which the Co nanoparticles were well dispersed on the graphene sheets ( Fig. 2). The average particle size of nanoparticle was about 35 nm. The lattice spacing was 0.201 nm on the HRTEM image, which corresponds to the (111) crystal face of Co nanocrystals. The results of elemental mapping analysis revealed that Co-His-GQD-G contains Co and N elements. Because Co element only comes from Co nanocrystals, and N element only comes from His-GQD, the result also confirms that the synthesis achieves the hybrid of Co nanocrystals and His-GQD.

There are four diffraction peaks at 26°, 44.2°, 51.5° and 75.6° on the XRD pattern in Fig. s1A. The peak at 26° is corresponded to the (002) crystal face of graphene. Due to the high content and poor crystallinity of graphene in the hybrid, the diffraction peak displays a strong and wide characteristic. The peaks at 44.2°, 51.5° and 75.6° are assigned to the Co (111), Co (200) and Co (220) crystal faces of Co nanocrystals. These are well consistent with the standard card of cobalt nanocrystal (JCPDS NO. 15–0806). In addition, the (220) crystal face of Co-His-GQD-G exhibits higher intensity than that of common Co nanoparticles (Li et al., 2018). The Raman spectrum include the G and D peaks of typical graphene materials. The G-band is a feature of the graphite layer, while the D-band corresponds to the disordered carbon or defective graphite structure. The investigation demonstrates the Raman spectrum is sensitive to subtle changes in the structure of carbon materials. A high ratio of the intensity of G peak to the intensity of D peak (ID/IG) indicates a large number of surface defects. This suggests that the graphene sheets were modified with N atoms (Zheng et al., 2014). From Fig. s1B, the ID/IG value was calculated. The ID/IG of 1.02 indicates that the nanohybrid has a high degree of graphitization.

The magnetic property of Co-His-GQD-G was studied, and the magnetization curve is shown in Fig. s2. It can be seen from Fig. s2A that the saturation magnetization value of Co-His-GQD-G was 23.54 emu g−1 when the external magnetic field was increased to 30,000 Oe. The saturation magnetization value indicates that the nanohybrid can be used to magnetic removal strategies, as shown in the insert of Fig. s2A. At the same time, it can be found from Fig. s2B that there was very slight hysteresis in the hysteresis loop, and the values of coercive field and remanant magnetization are 50 Oe and 4.5 emu g−1, respectively. This indicates that Co-His-GQD exhibits quasi-superparamagnetic behavior (McEvoy and Zhang, 2014). Moreover, the observed low coercive field and remanant magnetization also mean that Co-His-GQD-G can be prevented from agglomerating, and it can be easily redispersed for recovery or reuse in suspension.

The total, C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and Co 2p XPS spectra of Co-His-GQD-G were presented at Fig. 3. The total XPS spectrum demonstrates that the Co-His-GQD-G is mainly composed of C, N, O and Co elements. The C 1 s XPS spectrum includes three peaks at 283.4, 283.8 and 286.9 eV, corresponding to the C-C, C-O and C=O bonds. The N 1 s XPS spectrum gives four peaks, namely pyridine N (395.1 eV), pyrrole N (397.4 eV), graphite N (400.1 eV) and Co-N (398.9 eV). The above result verifies that graphite nitrogen has been inserted into the graphite plane and bonded with three carbon atoms. Because the pyrrole and pyridine-nitrogen atoms can improve electron density of graphene and open the band gap (Guo et al., 2012), Co-His-GQD-G can accelerate electron transfer to oxygen and leads to an excellent catalytic activity (Zhou et al., 2015). The O 1 s XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 529.4 and 531.4 eV, corresponding to O-C and O=C, respectively. The Co 2p XPS spectrum includes three peaks at 779.1, 794.6 and 783.9 eV. The peaks at 779.1 and 794.6 eV belong to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively. And the peak at 783.9 eV was corresponded to Co–Nx complexes, which was usually observed for Co-containing N-doped carbon materials (Zhang et al., 2017a). In addition, His-GQD, as the zero dimensional semiconducting graphene, will form a van der Waals heterojunction with 2D graphene (Yan et al., 2020). It can be seen from Fig. 3F that the peak of C 1 s XPS spectrum of Co-His-GQD-G was obviously shifted to the direction of lower binding energy compared with pure graphene. It indicates that the charge is transferred from His-GQD to graphene. The rapid charge transfer between graphene and His-GQD will improve the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G.

TMB was employed as a chromogenic substrate for evaluating the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G. Fig. 4 shows that no obvious color change can be observed in single TMB or Co-His-GQD-G reaction solution. The absorbance at 652 nm was close to zero in the absence of Co-His-GQD-G. However, the colorless reaction solution rapidly turns to blue after the Co-His-GQD-G was added into the mixture of TMB and acetate buffer. This verifies that the Co-His-GQD-G offers a high catalytic activity for oxidation of TMB. The high catalytic activity is mainly due to the small size of Co nanoparticles, the three-dimensional structure and high electronic conductivity of Co-His-GQD-G. Small size makes the catalyst fully contact with TMB, and three-dimensional structure and high electronic conductivity improve the electron/ion transfer. These factors make Co-His-GQD-G has excellent catalytic activity and bring an enhanced absorbance at 652 nm.

To understand the origin of catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G, the catalytic ability of Co-His-GQD-G in N2/air/O2 saturated solution was studied, and the results are shown in Fig. 5A. Notely, the absorbance at 652 nm in air and O2-saturated solution was significantly higher than that in N2-saturated solution. This exhibits that O2 plays a pivotal role in the reaction process. This proves that Co-His-GQD-G has intrinsic oxidase-like activity. The oxidase-like activity may be derived from the catalytic ability of Co-His-GQD-G for activation of O2 to generate reactive oxygen species (such as O2 ·-,

1O2,·OH) in the TMB oxidation reaction (Wang et al., 2020b). To study the catalytic mechanism, a series of experiments were carried out to identify possible reaction intermediates. p-Benzoquinone, NaN3 and tert-butylalcohol (TBA) were used as the scavenging agents to track superoxide radical (O2 ·-), singlet oxygen atom (1O2) and hydroxyl radical (·OH), respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, the addition of p-benzoquinone and NaN3 significantly reduced the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G, while TBA only has less reduced on the catalytic activity. In addition, the ESR technology was employed to further investigate the existence of O2 ·-, 1O2 and ·OH, coupled with 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidine (TEMP) as spin-trapping reagents for the capture of O2 ·- or ·OH, and 1O2, respectively (Sun et al., 2020). No obvious ESR signal was observed in Fig. s4A, indicating that the amount of ·OH in Co-His-GQD-G aqueous suspension was low. The ESR spectrum presents four peaks with an intensity of 1:1:1:1 when Co-His-GQD-G was dispersed in methanol solution (Fig. s4B). It suggests that the generation of O2 ·- in the Co-His-GQD-G methanol suspension. Singlet oxygen (1O2) was captured by TEMP to form 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine 1-oxyl (TEMPOL), which generated the triplet EPR peaks of equal intensity, suggesting the production of 1O2 in the Co-His-GQD-G aqueous suspension (Fig. s4C). This indicates that the Co-His-GQD-G can catalyze the activation of oxygen and mainly produce reactive oxygen species intermediate O2 ·- and 1O2. In brief, Co-His-GQD-G acts as catalyst to activate dissolved oxygen in Co-His-GQD-G-TMB system into O2 ·- and 1O2, and then the colorless TMB was oxidized to blue ox-TMB by the strong oxidation of active oxygen.

To test the influence of different components in Co-His-GQD-G on the catalytic activity, the absorbance at 652 nm of reaction system using Co-His-GQD-G, Co-His-GQD, His-GQD or graphene as the catalyst for oxidation of TMB was studied. In the experiment, a bigger absorbance represents a higher catalytic activity. Fig. 6A and B show that the absorbance at 652 nm of the reaction system with His-GQD does not change significantly with the increase of TMB, indicating that His-GQD lacks oxidase-like activity. However, the absorbance of the reaction system with graphene increased slightly. Meanwhile, the absorbance of the reaction system with Co-His-GQD was obviously increased. This may be due to the synergy between Co nanoparticle and His-GQD. The reaction system with Co-His-GQD-G gives the biggest absorbance among all reaction systems. This indicates that Co-His-GQD-G offers the best catalytic activity. This will accelerate the oxidation of TMB and leads to an increase absorbance. Further, the effect of Co-His-GQD-G concentration and incubation time on the oxidase-like activity of catalyst was further studied (shown in Fig. s3). To test the kinetic behaviour of the catalytic process, the apparent steady-state kinetic parameters of the catalytic reaction was determined by changing the concentration of TMB. According to the Lineweaver Burk equation (Fig. 6C and D), the Michaelis Menten constant (KM) and the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) were calculated to be 0.66 mM and 3.8 × 10−7 M s−1, respectively. These values are lower than many other nanomaterials in Table 1. Generally, a lower KM value indicates a higher affinity between the nanozyme and the substrate. This means that Co-His-GQD-G possess superior affinity toward TMB.

The high catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G is due to the following factors: (1) the rigid structure of graphene and the small size of Co nanoparticles make the catalytic active sites of catalyst fully exposed; (2) the high-index crystal face of nanoparticles was retained, which improve the catalytic activity of hybrid; (3) the well-defined three-dimensional graphene framework structure of the hybrid not only enables the rapid diffusion of dissolved oxygen to the catalytic active center, but also significantly improves electron transfer efficiency of the reaction system; (4) the doped graphite nitrogen in the hybrid makes the surface charge distribution uneven, which makes Co-His-GQD-G has excellent adsorption and reduction ability of O2 (Zhang et al., 2017b); (5) the van der Waals heterojunction formed between graphene and His-GQD can accelerate the charge transfer and improve the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G.

Based on the high catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G on oxidation of TMB, one sensing scheme was designed for colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos (shown in Fig. 7). In the scheme, Co-His-GQD-G catalyzes oxidation of TMB to ox-TMB in air due to its high catalytic activity and leads to an increased absorbance at 652 nm. A higher catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G brings a bigger absorbance at 652 nm. However, the absorbance at 652 nm will decrease in the presence of AChE and ATCh. This is because AChE catalyzes the hydrolysis of ATCh to produce thiocholine (TCh). The sulfhydryl (-SH) in TCh blocks the catalytic active sites on the surface of Co-His-GQD-G by coordination bonds between -SH and Co atom on the surface of Co nanoparticles. The action reduces the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G for TMB oxidation (Wu et al., 2019). As a result, the absorbance of ox-TMB strongly depends on catalytic activity of AChE. A higher activity of AChE shows a lower absorbance at 652 nm. In the presence of chlorpyrifos, chlorpyrifos can inhibit catalytic activity of AChE to hydrolysis of ATCh. Thus, a higher concentration of chlorpyrifos finally results in a higher catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G towards oxidation of TMB and an enhanced absorbance at 652 nm. The above results have demonstrated that the absorbance at 652 nm only depends on the chlorpyrifos concentration in the system when the amounts of ATCh, Co-His-GQD-G and TMB were fixed. Therefore, the proposed scheme can be used for colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos.

To examine the feasibility of the proposed method for detection of chlorpyrifos, the effects of Co-His-GQD-G, AChE and chlorpyrifos on the absorbance at 652 nm were studied, respectively. Fig. 8 shows that in the absence of any catalyst the absorbance at 652 nm was close to zero. This verifies that the oxidation of TMB to ox-TMB in air is very slow in the absence of catalyst. However, the absorbance will increase significantly in the presence of Co-His-GQD-G. This is attributed to the excellent catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G towards oxidation of TMB to ox-TMB. The catalysis accelerates the oxidation of TMB into ox-TMB and leads to an increased absorbance. The absorbance obviously decreases when AChE and ATCh were introduced into the system. Because the thiocholine produced by the hydrolysis of ATCh under catalysis of AChE inhibits the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G. The reduced catalytic activity results in a decreased oxidation of TMB, indicating a reduced absorbance at 652 nm. However, the absorbance will increase when chlorpyrifos was added into the reaction system. This is because chlorpyrifos can inhibit the catalytic activity of AChE. As a result, the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G was higher than that without chlorpyrifos. The enhanced activity brings a faster oxidation rate of TMB and leads to an increase in the absorbance at 652 nm.

To optimize the reaction conditions for detection of chlorpyrifos, the effects of ATCh concentration and incubation time on the absorbance at 652 nm caused by chlorpyrifos were investigated. Fig. 9A presents the relationship curve between ATCh concentration with the change in the absorbance at 652 nm (ΔA) caused by chlorpyrifos. The ΔA value is the difference between the absorbance at 652 nm without and with chlorpyrifos. With the increase of ATCh concentration, the ΔA value will rapidly increase when the ATCh concentration was less than 1 mM. A low concentration of ATCh, the inhibition of thiocholine towards the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G can not be fully exerted. Although the addition of chlorpyrifos reduced the hydrolysis ability of AChE to ATCh, the AChE is still sufficient to catalyze the hydrolysis of such a low concentration ATCh because the AChE is excessive relative to the ATCh. Therefore, the inhibition of chlorpyrifos on the enzyme activity of AChE could not be absolutely reflected in the change in the absorbance at 652 nm. When the concentration of ATCh was more than 1 mM, the ΔA value reaches to its maximum and then tends to be stable. This is because the catalytic activity of AChE is inhibited in the presence of chlorpyrifos. The catalytic ability of AChE reached the maximum when the concentration of ATCh was 1 mM. Therefore, ΔA value did not change with the increase of the concentration of ATCh. To obtain a high sensitivity, the ATCh concentration of 1 mM was employed for colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos.

Fig. 9B presents the relationship curve of incubation time between AChE and chlorpyrifos with the -ΔA value. It can be seen from Fig. 9B that the -ΔA value will increase rapidly with the increase of incubation time when the incubation time was less than 40 min. This is because the activity of AChE gradually decreases with the increase of incubation time. A longer incubation time brings a greater inhibition to the activity of AChE, and the higher catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G. For the reason, the inhibition rate of TCh towards the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G depends on the enzyme activity of AChE and incubation time. In this case, the inhibition of chlorpyrifos on the activity of AChE can be correctly reflected in the change of the absorbance at 652 nm of reaction system. When the incubation time was more than 40 min, the -ΔA value of the reaction system reached the maximum and then tends to be constant. This is because the inhibition to the activity AChE is close to the dynamic equilibrium. In the equilibrium state, the inhibition of TCh on the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G reaches to the maximum. At this point, the absorbance at 652 nm only depends on the chlorpyrifos in the system, and the -ΔA value does not increase with the increase of incubation time. To save the detection time and obtain a high sensitivity, the incubation time of 40 min was used for the colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos.

To verify the feasibility of the proposed method, the absorbance at 652 nm of the reaction system were measured in different concentration of chlorpyrifos and the results were shown in Fig. 10A. The absorbance at 652 nm will gradually increases with the increase of chlorpyrifos concentration. This is because the high concentration of chlorpyrifos finally leads to a more increase in the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G. This will produce more ox-TMB and indicates a bigger absorbance at 652 nm. Further, Fig. 10B demonstrates the relationship curve between the absorbance at 652 nm (A) and the concentration of chlorpyrifos (CChlorpyrifos). There was a good linear relationship between A and CChlorpyrifos when the concentration of chlorpyrifos was in the range of 2–20 ng mL−1. The correlation linear regression equation was A = 0.0087 × CChlorpyrifos+ 0.1001 with the correlation coefficient was 0.993, and an detection limit low to 0.57 ng mL−1 (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the illustration of Fig. 10A shows the corresponding photographs of the reaction solution taken under daylight. As can be seen, the color difference of the reaction solution can be clearly observed by the naked eyes. The recognizable diﬀerence by naked eyes could be reached down to a minimum concentration was 8 ng mL−1. In addition, the constructed colorimetric platform was compared with the detection of organophosphorus pesticides previously reported in other literature (shown in Table 2). It is clearly found that the linear range and detection limit of this method were better than other methods. The reproducibility of the method was verified by the determination of thirty samples of the same chlorpyrifos concentration (10 ng mL−1). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the experimental results was 3.12%, showing good reproducibility.

Selective recognition ability is an important feature to ensure the recognition accuracy and reduce the cross activity of sensing strategies, especially the construction of enzyme-triggered biosensors (Yan et al., 2019). The selectivity of this method for detection of chlorpyrifos was evaluated by studying some electrolytes and biological species in peach, including glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), glutamine (Gln), asparagine (Asn), tyrosine (Tyr) and ions (Mg2+, K+). Fig. 11 indicates 20 ng mL−1 of other substances studied causes a negligible change in the absorbance at 652 nm. The anti-interference capability of this approach for chlorpyrifos was also evaluated with these interference substances. As shown in Fig. s5, the effect of other substances on chlorpyrifos detection was negligible even at concentrations 50 times higher than that of chlorpyrifos. These demonstrate that the proposed analytical method is of good selectivity and anti-interference ability for detection of chlorpyrifos.

To explore the practicability of sensing strategy, a rapid and high-throughput method for colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos based on the Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet was developed. The Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet was prepared by dispersing Co-His-GQD-G magnetic nanohybrid into agarose hydrogel. As shown in Fig. s6, the color of Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet was uniform. This indicates that Co-His-GQD-G was dispersed equally in agarose gel sheet. The assay process of chlorpyrifos was shown in Fig. 12A. The reaction solution, composed of chlorpyrifos, AChE, ATCh, TMB and acetate buffer solution, was added to the microwell plate. The Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet was placed in the reaction solution by the magnetic interaction between electromagnet and magnetic gel sheet. After the incubation at room temperature, the gel sheet in the reaction solution was removed with electromagnet. The concentration of chlorpyrifos in the sample solution was determined by measuring the absorbance of reaction solution.

Reusability is one of the important factors to evaluate the stability of catalysts. The reusability of Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet was investigated by its application for the cyclic experiments of chlorpyrifos determination. Piror the next use, the magnetic gel sheet was washed with ethanol for several times to remove the absorbed ox-TMB. As shown in Fig. 12B, the catalytic activity of Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet was not obviously decreased (more than 95%) after 10 times of recycling. Futhermore, a series of characterizations for the Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet after 10 times of recycling were carried out. Fig. s7 shows that the appearance of magnetic hydrogel was not significantly different from before and after the cycling experiment. There is no obvious difference in the XRD spectrum of Co-His-GQD-G before and after recycling. The proves that the crystallinity of Co-His-GQD-G does not change (Fig. s8). Comparing the total XPS spectra of Co-His-GQD-G before and after the cycling experiment (Fig. 3A and Fig. s9A), we noted that the XPS spectra of Co-His-GQD-G after cycling appears one S 2p peak. This is because the AChE catalyzes the hydrolysis of ATCh to produce thiocholine in the reaction solution. The resulted sulfhydryl (-SH) in thiocholine, as Lewis bases, tends to coordinate with Co atom on the surface of Co nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. s9B, the high resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p include three peaks, namely Co 2p3/2 (778.5 eV), Co 2p1/2 (794.0 eV) and Co-N (783.3 eV), which were similar to those in the fresh catalyst. Compared with new Co-His-GQD-G, the XPS spectrum of Co 2p after cyclic experiment was shifted about 0.6 eV toward lower binding energy. This is because the coordination of Co atom with sulfhydryl group causes an energy transfer process from S atom to Co atom (Cui et al., 2018). In brief, these indicate that Co-His-GQD-G gel sheet has excellent reusability and mechanical stability.

To further investigate the applicability of Co-His-GQD-G magnetic gel sheet in real sample analysis, the concentration of chlorpyrifos in peaches was determined. The experimental results were shown in Table 3. The recovery of sensing system was range from 98.5% to 101.2%, which was within the recovery range allowed by China National Standard (GB/T 27404–2008). In addition, the gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry was also used to detection of the chlorpyrifos concentration in the sample (Kang et al., 2020). The results were consistent with the determination results of the proposed sensing system. This demonstrates that the proposed method has good accuracy and reproducibility. It has a potential application prospect in the rapid field monitoring of chlorpyrifos in actual samples.

The study demonstrated synthesis of Co-histidine functionalized graphene quantum dot-graphene nanohybrid. The resulting nanohybrid offers excellent oxidase-like catalytic activity, stability and quasi-superparamagnetism. The colorimetric method based on the nanohybrid as metal nanozyme exhibits the advantage of sensitivity, rapidity and stability for detection of chlorpyrifos. The proposed method has been successfully applied in colorimetric detection of chlorpyrifos in peaches. Excellent reusability makes the Co-His-GQD-G magnetic agarose gel sheet can be conveniently used for connection detection of chlorpyrifos. The study also provides one promising approach for fabrication of various graphene-based metal nanozymes with high catalytic activity.

Li Nana: Investigation and Writing. Li Ruiyi: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Wang Qinsheng: Investigation. Yang Yongqiang: Investigation. Sun Xiulan: Methodology. Wang Guangli: Validation and supervision. Li Zaijun: Conceptualization and methodology.
